title: HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2 — Wire Format and Multiplexing

questions.question: How many bytes is the fixed-size HTTP/2 frame header?
questions.explanation: The HTTP/2 frame header is exactly 9 bytes: 3 bytes for length, 1 byte for type, 1 byte for flags, and 4 bytes for the stream ID.

questions.question: What problem does HTTP/2 multiplexing solve that HTTP/1.1 pipelining could not?
options: Head-of-line blocking at the application layer
No support for chunked transfer encoding
questions.explanation: HTTP/1.1 pipelining requires responses to be returned in order, causing head-of-line blocking. HTTP/2 multiplexes independent streams on a single TCP connection, so responses can arrive in any order.

questions.question: What does HPACK provide in HTTP/2?
options: Header compression using static and dynamic tables
questions.explanation: HPACK is a compression format specifically designed for HTTP/2 header fields. It uses a combination of static and dynamic tables to reduce header size.

questions.question: In HTTP/1.1, what header signals that the body length is determined by chunk boundaries rather than Content-Length?
questions.explanation: Transfer-Encoding: chunked indicates the body is sent in chunks, each prefixed with its size in hexadecimal. The final chunk has size 0.

questions.question: What problem does HTTP/2's multiplexing solve?
options: It allows multiple requests/responses over a single TCP connection simultaneously, eliminating head-of-line blocking at the HTTP layer
It reduces the number of TCP connections to zero
questions.explanation: HTTP/1.1 is text-based and requires requests to be serialized on a connection (or opening many connections). HTTP/2 uses binary framing and multiplexes multiple streams on one TCP connection. A slow response doesn't block others — but TCP-level packet loss still blocks all streams.

_meta.title: Http 1 1 Http 2 Wire Format And Multiplexing
